title: 1.1.1. | The Story Problem | subheading

1.1.1. | The Story Problem | subheading


Story

, Mckee: Chapter 1


Robert McKee’s Story begins with a core assertion:

“Given the choice between trivial material brilliantly told versus profound material badly told, an audience will always choose the trivial told brilliantly.”

This is a powerful lesson: the success of a story is not determined by its subject matter, but by the way it is structured, executed, and delivered.

Whiplash is the perfect example of this principle in action—a story about a student drummer at a conservatory could be mundane in the wrong hands. But through masterful escalation, conflict, and structure, it becomes one of the most intense psychological thrillers of the decade.

1. Execution is More Important than Concept

McKee argues that great storytelling is about craft, not just ideas.

“A great story is not just a series of interesting events—it is a sequence of meaningful choices and consequences.”
